explainer.summary: God's anger burns against Israel (for reasons unstated), and He incites David to take a census. Even Joab protests, but David insists. After the census, David is struck with guilt. God offers three punishments; David chooses plague, trusting God's mercy over human cruelty. Seventy thousand die before David's intercession and sacrifice at Araunah's threshing floor stop the plague.
themes: The danger of trusting numbers over God
The origin of the temple site
explainer.contextInBook: This final chapter establishes the site of the future temple. David's purchase of Araunah's threshing floor provides the ground where Solomon will build the house of God. The book that began with David receiving Saul's kingdom ends with David purchasing the most sacred site in Israel's future history.

wordStudies.translatedAs: God 'moved' or 'incited' David to take the census,
wordStudies.meaning: God 'moved' or 'incited' David to take the census, while 1 Chronicles 21:1 says Satan incited him. Both are true in Hebrew thought: God uses secondary agents (including Satan) to accomplish His purposes. The tension reflects the mystery of divine sovereignty and human responsibility.

wordStudies.transliteration: I will not offer... that which costs me nothing
wordStudies.translatedAs: David refuses to offer sacrifice that costs him no
wordStudies.meaning: David refuses to offer sacrifice that costs him nothing (chinam = free, for nothing). True worship requires personal cost -- giving God something that represents genuine sacrifice, not leftovers or charity.

verses.text: Again Yahweh’s anger burned against Israel, and he moved David against them, saying, “Go, count Israel and Judah.”
verses.explainer: Yahweh's anger burns against Israel and He incites David to number the people. The tension between divine sovereignty and human responsibility is left unresolved.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The tension between this verse (God incited David) and 1 Chronicles 21:1 (Satan incited David) reflects the Hebrew understanding that God can use secondary agents, including adversarial ones, to accomplish His sovereign purposes. Both are true simultaneously.

crossReferences.connection: The parallel account says Satan incited David, showing the complementary Hebrew understanding that God works through secondary agents

verses.text: The king said to Joab the captain of the army, who was with him, “Now go back and forth through all the tribes of Israel, from Dan even to Beersheba, and count the people, that I may know the sum of the people.”
verses.explainer: David orders Joab to conduct a census of Israel, counting the fighting men from Dan to Beersheba. The census was apparently sinful, though the exact reason is debated.

verses.text: Joab said to the king, “Now may Yahweh your God add to the people, however many they may be, one hundred times; and may the eyes of my lord the king see it. But why does my lord the king delight in this thing?”
verses.explainer: Even Joab protests, asking why David wants to do this. When Joab -- not known for moral sensitivity -- questions an order, the reader should take notice.

annotations.word: why does my lord the king delight in this thing?

verses.text: Notwithstanding, the king’s word prevailed against Joab, and against the captains of the army. Joab and the captains of the army went out from the presence of the king to count the people of Israel.
verses.explainer: The king's word overrides Joab's objection. David insists, and Joab and the army captains go out to conduct the count.

verses.text: They passed over the Jordan, and encamped in Aroer, on the right side of the city that is in the middle of the valley of Gad, and to Jazer;
verses.explainer: The census begins east of the Jordan at Aroer, moving through Gad and up to Jazer. The systematic coverage shows the thoroughness of the count.

verses.text: then they came to Gilead, and to the land of Tahtim Hodshi; and they came to Dan Jaan, and around to Sidon,
verses.explainer: They proceed to Gilead, the land of the Hittites (Kadesh), Dan, Sidon -- covering the entire northern extent of David's territory.

verses.text: and came to the stronghold of Tyre, and to all the cities of the Hivites, and of the Canaanites; and they went out to the south of Judah, at Beersheba.
verses.explainer: They reach Tyre and all the Hivite and Canaanite cities, then south to Beersheba. The count covers the traditional boundaries of Israel.

annotations.word: came to the stronghold of Tyre

verses.text: So when they had gone back and forth through all the land, they came to Jerusalem at the end of nine months and twenty days.
verses.explainer: The census takes nine months and twenty days -- nearly ten months to cover the entire kingdom. The scale of the operation reflects the kingdom's vast size under David.

verses.text: Joab gave up the sum of the counting of the people to the king: and there were in Israel eight hundred thousand valiant men who drew the sword; and the men of Judah were five hundred thousand men.
verses.explainer: The final count: 800,000 fighting men in Israel and 500,000 in Judah -- 1.3 million total. These numbers may represent military potential rather than an exact count.

annotations.word: eight hundred thousand valiant men who drew the sword

verses.text: David’s heart struck him after he had counted the people. David said to Yahweh, “I have sinned greatly in that which I have done. But now, Yahweh, put away, I beg you, the iniquity of your servant; for I have done very foolishly.”
verses.explainer: David's heart strikes him after the census. He confesses to God: 'I have sinned greatly.' Repentance follows immediately upon recognition of sin.

verses.text: When David rose up in the morning, Yahweh’s word came to the prophet Gad, David’s seer, saying,
verses.explainer: The prophet Gad comes to David the next morning with God's message. David's 'seer' becomes the instrument of divine judgment.

verses.text: “Go and speak to David, ‘Yahweh says, “I offer you three things. Choose one of them, that I may do it to you.”’”
verses.explainer: God offers David three options -- three judgments from which he must choose. The choice itself is both a mercy (David gets to choose) and a judgment (all options are terrible).

verses.text: So Gad came to David, and told him, and said to him, “Shall seven years of famine come to you in your land? Or will you flee three months before your foes while they pursue you? Or shall there be three days’ pestilence in your land? Now answer, and consider what answer I shall return to him who sent me.”
verses.explainer: The three options: seven years of famine, three months of military defeat, or three days of plague. Each option scales differently in duration and source of suffering.

verses.text: David said to Gad, “I am in distress. Let us fall now into Yahweh’s hand; for his mercies are great. Let me not fall into man’s hand.”
verses.explainer: David chooses plague, saying 'Let us fall into the hand of God, for His mercies are great; let me not fall into the hand of man.' He trusts divine mercy over human cruelty.

thenVsNow.then: David's choice reveals deep theological wisdom: in judgment, God's character includes mercy, while human enemies have no such constraint. Better to be punished by a loving God than defeated by a merciless enemy.
thenVsNow.now: David's choice reveals deep theological wisdom: in judgment, God's character includes mercy, while human enemies have no such constraint. Better to be punished by a loving God than defeated by a merciless enemy.

verses.text: So Yahweh sent a pestilence on Israel from the morning even to the appointed time; and seventy thousand men died of the people from Dan even to Beersheba.
verses.explainer: Yahweh sends a plague from morning until the appointed time. Seventy thousand people die across Israel -- the most devastating single event in David's reign.

verses.text: When the angel stretched out his hand toward Jerusalem to destroy it, Yahweh relented of the disaster, and said to the angel who destroyed the people, “It is enough. Now withdraw your hand.” Yahweh’s angel was by the threshing floor of Araunah the Jebusite.
verses.explainer: The destroying angel reaches Jerusalem, and God relents. He tells the angel to stop at the threshing floor of Araunah the Jebusite.

annotations.word: the threshing floor of Araunah the Jebusite

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The threshing floor of Araunah on Mount Moriah becomes the site of Solomon's temple (2 Chronicles 3:1). The place where judgment was halted by mercy becomes the place where God dwells among His people -- a profound theological connection between atonement and worship.

crossReferences.connection: Solomon builds the temple on this exact site -- the threshing floor of Araunah/Ornan where the plague was stopped

verses.text: David spoke to Yahweh when he saw the angel who struck the people, and said, “Behold, I have sinned, and I have done perversely; but these sheep, what have they done? Please let your hand be against me, and against my father’s house.”
verses.explainer: David sees the destroying angel and intercedes: let the punishment fall on me and my family, not on the innocent people. He accepts personal responsibility for a sin that caused public suffering.

annotations.word: I have sinned, and I have done perversely; but these sheep, what have they done?

verses.text: Gad came that day to David, and said to him, “Go up, build an altar to Yahweh on the threshing floor of Araunah the Jebusite.”
verses.explainer: Gad instructs David to build an altar on Araunah's threshing floor. The plague's stopping point becomes the site of worship and sacrifice.

verses.text: David went up according to the saying of Gad, as Yahweh commanded.
verses.explainer: David goes up to the threshing floor as God commanded through Gad. Obedience follows immediately upon instruction.

verses.text: Araunah looked out, and saw the king and his servants coming on toward him. Then Araunah went out, and bowed himself before the king with his face to the ground.
verses.explainer: Araunah looks down and sees the king approaching with his servants. He goes out and prostrates himself before David.

verses.text:
Araunah said, “Why has my lord the king come to his servant?”
David said, “To buy your threshing floor, to build an altar to Yahweh, that the plague may be stopped from afflicting the people.”
verses.explainer: Araunah asks why the king has come. David explains he needs to buy the threshing floor to build an altar and stop the plague.

verses.text: Araunah said to David, “Let my lord the king take and offer up what seems good to him. Behold, the cattle for the burnt offering, and the threshing instruments and the yokes of the oxen for the wood.
verses.explainer: Araunah offers everything free: the threshing floor, oxen for sacrifice, wooden implements for fuel. He tries to give the king everything at no cost.

verses.text: All this, O king, does Araunah give to the king.” Araunah said to the king, “May Yahweh your God accept you.”
verses.explainer: Araunah gives it all to the king, blessing him with the prayer that God accept his sacrifice. The Jebusite's generosity and theology are both impressive.

verses.text: The king said to Araunah, “No; but I will most certainly buy it from you for a price. I will not offer burnt offerings to Yahweh my God which cost me nothing.” So David bought the threshing floor and the oxen for fifty shekels of silver.
verses.explainer: David refuses the free gift: 'I will not offer burnt offerings to Yahweh my God which cost me nothing.' He buys the floor for fifty shekels of silver.

annotations.word: I will not offer burnt offerings to Yahweh my God which cost me nothing

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: David's insistence on paying full price establishes a principle of sacrificial worship: offerings must cost the giver something. Free-of-charge worship is not worship at all. This principle runs through biblical theology from Abraham's sacrifice of Isaac to God's sacrifice of His Son.

crossReferences.connection: The parallel gives a much higher price (600 shekels of gold), possibly for the entire mountain rather than just the threshing floor area

thenVsNow.then: David's refusal to offer God something that cost him nothing establishes one of the Bible's most enduring principles of worship: genuine sacrifice must involve genuine cost.

verses.text: David built an altar to Yahweh there, and offered burnt offerings and peace offerings. So Yahweh was entreated for the land, and the plague was removed from Israel.
verses.explainer: David builds the altar, offers burnt offerings and peace offerings, and God responds to prayer for the land. The plague stops, and the book ends with worship.

annotations.word: Yahweh was entreated for the land, and the plague was removed from Israel

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: 2 Samuel ends not with David's death but with worship and mercy. The threshing floor where judgment stopped becomes the temple mount -- the place where God's presence dwells. From David's worst moment (the census) comes Israel's most sacred place.

thenVsNow.then: 2 Samuel ends where the temple will begin. The place of judgment becomes the place of worship, and the site of plague becomes the site of God's permanent dwelling -- a final demonstration that God brings redemption even from disaster.
